story.title: Dr. Al-Rashid's Virus-Busting Model
story.text:
Hello! I'm Dr. Fatima Al-Rashid. I'm an engineer. My job is to design special surfaces that destroy germs on contact. Think of a door handle that cleans itself! I study tiny invaders called pathogens, like viruses. To understand how to stop them, I build models that show how different parts of a system work together.

Let’s make a model for handwashing. 1. First, we need soap. The amount of soap is important. We can call this Soap Concentration. 2. Next, we need water. Is Water Present? Yes! Water helps the soap spread everywhere. 3. When soap and water mix, they create Soap Effectiveness. This tells us how well the soap is working. More soap and water make the soap more effective.

So, how does soap work? 1. Viruses wear a protective coat made of fat. This is their lipid membrane. 2. Soap molecules are special. They are amphiphilic. This means one end of the molecule loves water, and the other end loves fat. 3. The soap molecule’s fat-loving end sticks into the virus's lipid membrane. It pulls the membrane apart like a zipper!

My model helps me measure the final result: the Virus Survival Rate. When soap effectiveness is high, the virus survival rate is very low. That's our goal! In my lab, I also test other things. Does Water Temperature matter? Or how hard you scrub? These are new parts we could add to our model.
think_abouts: Dr. Al-Rashid mentioned adding 'how hard you scrub' to the model. How would that connect to the other parts?
What is another part you think could be added to the handwashing model? (Hint: Think about how long you wash your hands.)

margin_defs.definition: Tiny germs that can make you sick.
The virus's outer coat made of fat.
A molecule that is attracted to both water and fat.

check_prompts: What two things make soap more effective?
What part of a virus does soap attack?
What happens to the virus survival rate when soap is effective?

vocabulary.definition: A fat or oil molecule that makes up the outer coating of a virus.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A virus is covered in a layer of lipid.

vocabulary.definition: A thin layer that surrounds and protects a cell or virus.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The soap breaks apart the virus's outer membrane.

vocabulary.definition: A special type of molecule that is attracted to both water AND oil/fat.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Soap is amphiphilic, so it can grab both water and oily dirt.

vocabulary.definition: Any tiny germ, like a virus or bacteria, that can cause disease.
vocabulary.use_sentence: We wash our hands to get rid of any dangerous pathogen.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Al-Rashid studies special surfaces that clean themselves. How is washing your hands with soap like using one of her special surfaces?
discussion_cards.frame: Washing my hands is like using a special surface because...

discussion_cards.prompt: Why is just using water not as good as using soap and water to destroy a virus?
discussion_cards.frame: Just using water isn't as good because the water needs soap to...

boxes.label: Cause
boxes.content: You add soap and water to your hands.

boxes.label: Effect
boxes.content: The soap molecules surround the virus.

boxes.label: Cause
boxes.content: The soap's 'fat-loving' end breaks the virus's lipid membrane.

boxes.label: Effect
boxes.content: The virus falls apart and is washed away.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
